title: Reflections on the Cost Bottlenecks of Advanced Packaging in the Next Three Years: Is Chiplet Truly the Silver Bullet?
author: Daniel Nenni
text:
K

Kieu
New member

Hello fellow semiconductor professionals and technical experts,

As the cost-efficiency of Moore’s Law slows at advanced process nodes, Chiplet and 3D IC technologies are increasingly viewed by the industry as the core path to sustaining compute scaling. Chiplets have been a hot topic at recent technical symposia, appearing almost as a “silver bullet” for all challenges in heterogeneous integration.

However, as a practitioner with a long-term focus on backend and package design, I have observed some undeniable “reefs” that we are encountering as we push toward large-scale commercialization. While Chiplets excel in system design flexibility and area efficiency, several practical challenges seem to be offsetting the anticipated cost benefits:

KGD (Known Good Die) Testing and Quality Assurance: In multi-die integration, the yield of individual dies dictates the final package yield. How can we ensure that all Chiplets are "Known Good" before interconnection using more economical methods? Is testing cost becoming the new "hidden cost center"?
Thermal Management and the Power Wall: With increased integration density, the constraints imposed by thermal coupling on system reliability and performance ceilings are becoming increasingly apparent. For high-power AI acceleration chips, are our current thermal design solutions beginning to reach their limits?
Interconnect Process Yield and Collaboration: Complex interconnect processes (such as CoWoS, TSV, etc.) demand extreme precision and environmental control, making yield management still the greatest uncertainty in mass production.
Given these challenges, I would like to turn to the experts in this forum:

In your actual project implementations, what do you consider to be the biggest "stumbling block" currently hindering the large-scale commercialization of Chiplets?

Is it the immaturity of the EDA toolchain in multi-physics co-optimization? Is it the capacity premiums and high Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) costs from OSATs? Or perhaps the lack of a unified die-to-die interface standard in the ecosystem?

I look forward to hearing your insights as we explore these "growing pains" together.
